name: Model Pruning
transcript:
So in pruning, as the name suggests, it is very simple. All you need to do is remove certain things from the network. So here are the weights. So unstructured pruning is like randomly keeping removing weights from the network. With the hope that you don't affect performance. And structure pruning is more like what you take into account. The exact structure of the network that you are going to prune. and then prune certain structural components from the network.

So, I will cover both of them. So, the simplest form of unstructured pruning is called magnitude pruning. Just sort the weights; whichever weights are low, just ditch them. It is as simple as that. So, what they found is that if you remove 40 percent of the weight, You do not actually see that bigger change in performance. But if you start removing a lot more, then you see degradation. But what they say is that after you prune, if you fine-tune, continue pre-training. Or continue fine-tuning; then you would end up bridging that gap.

Until 80% of the cases. And some people figured out that everybody only does weight pruning. You only take into account the weight. Of the parameters, only the parameters are important, but activations are also important. So, if you take into account activations, then the product of weights is and activations will show you a different story. So, rather than pruning at the weight level, prune at the activations. So, this is one idea that was very recently proposed. And so the issue with unstructured pruning is that, let's say you prune,

You make a lot of values in your matrix. But still, if hardware is not going to utilize that information, It is as much about using that same FP16 to store 0 as well. It is not going to give you a speed up in numbers. It is not going to save compute; it is not going to save energy. Nothing is saved. So there is no point in actually doing unstructured pruning. If your hardware does not support it, then it is a very important part. So, people started to move slightly away from unstructured pruning.

And start to focus on structured pruning. For example, there are these A100 NVIDIA GPUs which say that If you take up any block of four, you compress it. into blocks of 2 by ignoring 2. This is called a 2-to-4 sparsity pattern. If you do that and represent your values with half the number of weights. and you use 2-bit indices to store the indices that are non-zero, It will give you a performance speedup. So, now your tuning algorithm that you are going to design is ready. Should be aware of this particular hardware choice as a sort of knowledge test.

So, if you see here, if you do it for any different type of precision, The dense operation, and there is a big difference between. How much you can do with the dense operation and the sparse operations. So, because the hardware supports this, Now you can think of pruning your weights in this particular format without loss. While minimizing the loss of performance. Another recent paper that came out was that. Let's say that you have a fine-tuned BERT; there are a lot of multi-head attentions.

Feed-forward networks and within multi-head attention, you have multiple heads. Within a feed-forward network, you would have certain hidden dimensions and so on. So what they say is that you can prune almost any component, right? You can remove a layer; for example, they remove this feedforward layer. If they remove this multi-head attention, you can remove certain heads. Within the multi-head, you can remove certain hidden dimensions here and there. And they say that you should take into account the structure of the network.

And remove structural components; you end up getting a performance speed-up. So, this is about pruning. So I am just covering the overview to give a brief picture of what pruning is. I am not going into details or other techniques that do pruning more efficiently. Finally, I will talk about distillation. So, distillation again is a process of training a much smaller model. to imitate a bigger model. The bigger model is the teacher, and then the smaller model is the student. So the smaller model should end up mimicking what the larger model does.

So it sort of works like this: One of the biggest issues with distillation is that you need this data. So, think about post-training quantization. If you have the LAMA 70 billion model and you want to quantize it, You don't need the data that LAMA was trained on. You don't have to find a proxy for the data for which Lama was trained. You can just do your quantization. But here, you need the data. If you don't have the real data, you should sort it out. end up getting some representative data.

That sort of looks like what this model would have trained on. And that is a big bottleneck in this era. Because people are willing to share and open-source their models. But nobody is willing to open-source the data because that's the goal. So, nobody would do that. So this distillation is typically difficult from that standpoint. And so, people do task-specific distillation. You have task-specific data; let me distill the model conditioned on the task.

name: Mixture of Experts Model
description:
### Summary  
The talk focuses on scaling transformer models through sparsity, particularly via the Switch Transformer architecture, which employs sparse mixtures of experts. The motivation arises from the established scaling laws that show larger models yield better performance and efficiency but are computationally expensive. Sparsity introduces a new dimension of scaling by selectively activating different expert weights for each input token, enabling massive parameter counts without proportional increases in computation.

The Switch Transformer simplifies previous mixture-of-experts (MoE) models by routing each token to a single expert rather than multiple, reducing communication overhead and improving efficiency. The talk covers key innovations needed to train such sparse models stably at scale, including selective precision casting (especially for router calculations), careful initialization, enhanced dropout regularization for experts to combat overfitting, and differentiable load balancing to evenly distribute tokens among experts.

Experiments show Switch Transformers achieve significant speedups—up to 7×—over dense T5 baselines during pre-training, with good scaling properties as expert count increases. Although sparse models have many more parameters, they maintain or improve sample efficiency and scale better than dense models with model parallelism alone. Sparse models also excel in multilingual and knowledge-intensive tasks, though they can sometimes underperform on reasoning-heavy benchmarks when extremely sparse.

The talk also discusses the engineering challenges of routing tokens with fixed expert capacities, and methods like “no token left behind” that were tested but found not to improve performance. Model distillation techniques are explored to compress large sparse models back into smaller dense counterparts for easier deployment, retaining about 30–40% of sparsity gains.

Lastly, the speakers address broader research questions about adaptive computation versus sparsity, hardware co-design for parallelism, and future directions including using sparsity in vision transformers and adaptive compute per input. Overall, the Switch Transformer presents a promising, efficient way to scale large transformer models with sparsity, balancing computational cost and model size.

### Highlights  
- [00:00:37] ⚙️ Introduction to scaling laws in neural language models and motivation for exploring sparsity.  
- [00:03:24] 🧠 Explanation of sparse mixtures of experts and routing tokens to specialized experts.  
- [00:06:28] 🚀 Switch Transformer routes each token to a single expert to reduce communication costs and simplify training.  
- [00:07:22] 🔧 Key training improvements: selective precision casting, smaller initialization, expert dropout, and differentiable load balancing.  
- [00:29:05] 📈 Switch Transformer scales with expert count, achieving up to 7× speedups over dense T5 baseline.  
- [00:42:48] ⚖️ Trade-offs between sparse model flops and parameters explored via Switch XL and Switch C models.  
- [01:00:02] 🌍 Sparse models show strong performance in multilingual settings and knowledge-intensive tasks.  

### Key Insights  
- [00:01:19] 🔍 **Scaling laws predict model performance and efficiency:** Larger models tend to be more sample efficient, enabling predictable improvements with scale. Sparsity adds a new dimension to scaling by increasing parameters without linear compute increase.  
  *Analysis:* This insight refines the traditional approach of scaling only model size or compute, suggesting sparsity enables ultra-large models that remain computationally feasible. It guides future architectures towards conditional computation.  

- [00:03:24] 🎯 **Mixture of Experts (MoE) effectively routes tokens to specialized subnetworks:** The router assigns each token a probability distribution over experts, enabling input-dependent computation.  
  *Analysis:* This conditional activation reduces wasted computation on irrelevant weights, improving parameter efficiency and potentially enabling expert specialization, which can enhance model capacity and interpretability.  

- [00:07:22] 🛠️ **Training sparse models requires novel techniques for stability and efficiency:** Selective precision (casting router ops to float32), reduced initialization scale, and dropout on expert layers mitigate instability and overfitting. Differentiable load balancing ensures hardware efficiency by evenly distributing tokens.  
  *Analysis:* Sparse models are more prone to instability due to dynamic routing and large parameter counts; these targeted methods are crucial for practical deployment and training at scale.  

- [00:13:39] 💡 **Capacity factor tuning balances token routing and communication overhead:** Lower capacity factors reduce communication and memory costs but risk token dropping; surprisingly, dropping tokens minimally harms performance, indicating robustness.  
  *Analysis:* This presents a trade-off in system design for sparse models, suggesting that hardware and algorithmic parameters must be co-optimized for best performance and efficiency.  

- [00:26:30] ⚖️ **Switch Transformer’s top-1 routing is more Pareto efficient than top-2 routing:** Although top-2 routing can yield slightly better quality, top-1 routing achieves faster training and lower communication costs, making it preferable in practice.  
  *Analysis:* Simplifying routing reduces overhead significantly, making sparse models more scalable and practical for large-scale training regimes.  

- [00:42:48] 🔍 **Sparse models can have trillions of parameters with fewer flops:** Switch C with 1.6 trillion parameters uses fewer flops than smaller sparse or dense models, trading off fewer computations for more parameters, which benefits knowledge-intensive tasks.  
  *Analysis:* This supports the hypothesis that parameters encode knowledge while flops relate to reasoning ability, influencing model design based on target downstream tasks.  

- [00:47:46] 🎓 **Distillation retains ~30–40% of sparse model gains in dense models:** Compressing sparse experts into dense models preserves a significant fraction of improvements, facilitating deployment where large sparse models are impractical.  
  *Analysis:* Distillation provides a practical pathway to use sparse model benefits during training while maintaining simpler architectures for inference and serving, key for production systems.  

- [01:00:52] 🔄 **Adaptive computation and sparsity can be combined:** Recent work in vision transformers shows that parts of inputs can be selectively processed, combining sparsity with variable compute per input.  
  *Analysis:* This suggests future models may dynamically allocate both different experts and compute amounts to inputs, improving efficiency and specialization further.  

- [01:04:34] 🧩 **Hardware-aware model and parallelism co-design is essential:** Models are designed to fit well with hardware mesh architectures and parallelism strategies, with internal dimensions tuned to optimize compute-to-memory ratios.  
  *Analysis:* Achieving high efficiency at scale requires tight integration between model architecture, distributed training methods, and hardware capabilities, an important area for future research.  

### Additional Notes  
- Sparse models offer a promising path to scale transformers beyond dense model limits.  
- Practical engineering challenges such as routing, token capacity, and precision casting are non-trivial but solvable.  
- Sparsity yields benefits especially in multilingual and knowledge-heavy applications rather than reasoning-heavy tasks.  
- Distillation and adaptive compute represent important future directions to balance model size, computation, and deployment constraints.
links: Stanford CS25: V1 I Mixture of Experts (MoE) paradigm and the Switch Transformer
transcript:
[00:00:05]  
### Introduction to Scaling Transformers via Sparsity  
- The talk introduces the concept of **scaling transformers through sparsity**, focusing on a form of sparsity where **each input token is processed by a different subset of weights or receives varied computation**.  
- The motivation stems from the community’s realization that **scale is a critical factor for strong model performance**, fueling an arms race among institutions to train larger models.  
- The foundational work referenced is the 2020 OpenAI paper on **scaling laws for neural language models**, which demonstrated that model performance scales predictably with size (parameters, compute) across orders of magnitude.  
- Larger models show **better sample efficiency**, meaning for a fixed compute budget, it's preferable to train larger models for fewer steps than smaller models for more steps.  
- Prior work focused on scaling dense models by increasing model dimensions, but sparsity is presented as a **new axis for architectural scaling**, where the model activates only certain weights per input token.

[00:02:39]  
### Overview of Sparse Mixture of Experts (MoE)  
- The concept of sparsity discussed traces back to the 1991 paper on **adaptive mixtures of local experts**.  
- Recently revisited by Noam Chazeir et al. at Google Brain, they replaced feed-forward networks in LSTMs with mixture of experts (MoE).  
- The MoE architecture involves multiple experts (small networks or dense matrices).  
- A **router network** produces a probability distribution (via softmax) over experts per token, selecting which experts to activate.  
- Outputs of selected experts are combined as a weighted mixture for final output.  
- MoEs have shown success in NLP tasks like translation but come with challenges such as training stability and communication costs, which the **Switch Transformer** paper addresses.

[00:05:01]  
### Switch Transformer Architecture and Training Improvements  
- The **Switch Transformer** simplifies MoE by routing each token to only the **single highest-probability expert**, reducing communication overhead and simplifying training.  
- In the Transformer architecture, every 2nd or 4th feed-forward layer is replaced by a switch layer.  
- Each switch layer’s router assigns tokens to one of several experts (weight matrices). Token inputs receive the same amount of floating-point operations but use **different sets of weights**, enabling adaptive computation by weight selection.  
- Key training improvements include:  
  - **Selective precision**: casting critical computations (especially router computations involving exponentiation) to higher precision (float32) while training the rest in lower precision (bfloat16) to ensure stability.  
  - **Initialization scale adjustment**: smaller initial weights improve stability and performance.  
  - **Increased dropout in expert layers**: combats overfitting, especially important since sparse models have more parameters and different fine-tuning dynamics.  
  - **Differentiable load balancing loss**: encourages roughly equal token distribution among experts to optimize hardware efficiency.

[00:09:08]  
### Selective Precision for Stable Training  
- Sparse models are more unstable than dense ones during training, often leading to divergence after thousands of steps.  
- The router’s softmax involves exponentiation, which is sensitive to floating-point precision errors.  
- Casting router computations to float32 mitigates instability without significant compute cost due to the router’s relatively small computation footprint.  
- This precision adjustment is essential for stable training of sparse models at scale.

[00:10:35]  
### Initialization and Overfitting Mitigation  
- Reducing initialization scale significantly improves sparse model training stability and quality.  
- Sparse models tend to overfit more during fine-tuning due to their larger parameter counts.  
- Increasing dropout rate specifically in expert layers is effective in reducing overfitting across multiple fine-tuning tasks.  
- Empirical results show improved fine-tuning robustness with these techniques.

[00:13:01]  
### Load Balancing and Routing Strategies  
- Load balancing is critical to ensure experts get a roughly equal number of tokens, which reduces communication overhead and maximizes hardware throughput.  
- The team experimented with **“no token left behind”** routing, which attempts multi-stage routing to avoid dropping tokens by routing dropped tokens to second-best experts.  
- Surprisingly, this approach **did not improve performance** and sometimes degraded it, suggesting that forcing tokens to less optimal experts harms performance.  
- Sparse models are robust to token dropping, especially during fine-tuning, and sometimes it is better to allow some token drops than to force load balancing at the cost of routing quality.  
- Load balancing loss coefficient tuning balances between encouraging balanced utilization and maintaining expert specialization.

[00:20:36]  
### Research Questions and Motivation  
- The overarching research question is whether models can be designed to perform **adaptive computation with different weights per input** rather than uniform dense computation.  
- Sparsity was chosen as a first step because it has shown empirical success and maps well to existing hardware and software infrastructures.  
- The goal is to build a framework for efficiently training sparse transformer variants as an alternative to dense models.  
- Adaptive computation with variable amounts of FLOPs per input is also important but more complex due to hardware and software constraints and remains an open research area.  
- Sparsity and adaptive computation are related but distinct: sparsity varies parameters used per input, adaptive computation varies computation amount (FLOPs).

[00:26:02]  
### Performance Comparisons: Top-1 vs. Top-2 Routing and Capacity Factor  
- **Capacity factor** defines how many tokens each expert can process relative to the average token load.  
- At higher capacity factors (e.g., 2.0), mixture of experts (MoE) models with top-2 routing outperform switch transformers (top-1 routing) because MoE routes tokens to multiple experts, increasing model capacity.  
- At lower capacity factors (e.g., 1.0 or 1.25), switch transformers outperform MoE models, showing better time-to-quality trade-offs due to reduced communication and compute overhead.  
- Lower capacity factors are desirable for efficiency, reducing communication costs and memory overhead, making switch transformers more **Pareto-optimal** in many cases.

[00:29:05]  
### Scaling Behavior of Switch Transformers  
- Increasing the number of experts from 1 to 128 steadily improves perplexity versus training steps, though with diminishing returns at very high expert counts.  
- When considering wall-clock training time (including communication overhead), switch transformers demonstrate up to **7x speedups over dense T5-base models**.  
- These speedups are significant in deep learning, indicating practical benefits of sparsity in pre-training large-scale models.

[00:31:12]  
### Scaling Laws and Parallelism Types  
- Sparse parameters (experts) increase model size without increasing FLOPs, yielding diminishing but consistent gains in performance with more experts.  
- Two main axes for scaling:  
  - **Expert parallelism (sparsity)**: increasing number of experts increases parameters but not FLOPs.  
  - **Model parallelism (dense)**: partitioning dense model weights across devices increases FLOPs and parameters.  
- Sparse models with expert parallelism achieve **2.5x speedups** over larger dense models using model parallelism, indicating sparsity as a more efficient scaling dimension.

[00:33:03]  
### Sparse Models with Small Number of Experts  
- Even with very few experts (e.g., 2), sparse models scale better than dense baselines, suggesting applicability in low-compute environments like single GPUs.  
- This indicates sparse architectures’ potential beyond massive clusters to more modest hardware.

[00:34:10]  
### Combining Parallelism Strategies and Hardware Considerations  
- Parallelism strategies reviewed:  
  - **Data parallelism:** replicates weights, partitions data.  
  - **Model parallelism:** partitions weights, replicates data.  
  - **Expert parallelism + data parallelism:** partitions experts across cores, replicates data similarly to data parallelism.  
- Switch Transformer uses a combination where experts are partitioned across cores and data is partitioned and replicated appropriately.  
- The **mesh transformer abstraction** supports these multi-dimensional parallelisms through SPMD (single program, multiple data) style tensor partitioning.  
- Effective hardware mapping requires balancing weight partitioning and communication costs.

[00:41:46]  
### Large Sparse Model Designs and Pre-training Results  
- Two large sparse models trained and compared to the largest dense T5-XXL:  
  - **Switch XL**: similar FLOPs per token as T5-XXL, 64 experts, ~400B parameters, better step-based performance than T5-XXL.  
  - **Switch C**: 1.6 trillion parameters, fewer FLOPs (almost 10x less than others), trades compute for more parameters.  
- Switch C shows worse step-based performance but better efficiency (TPU hours) on a Pareto frontier, indicating benefits of extreme sparsity with reduced compute.

[00:43:45]  
### Parameter vs. Compute Trade-offs in Sparse Models  
- Following Noam Chazeir’s hypothesis:  
  - **Parameters** contribute more to knowledge storage and reasoning.  
  - **Compute (FLOPs)** contributes more to intelligence (reasoning speed/ability).  
- Experiments analyzed fine-tuning on two tasks:  
  - **SuperGLUE** (reasoning task)  
  - **TriviaQA** (knowledge-heavy question answering)  
- For fixed pre-training perplexity:  
  - Sparse models underperform dense models on reasoning tasks (SuperGLUE).  
  - Sparse models outperform dense on knowledge-heavy tasks (TriviaQA).  
- Indicates sparse models encode knowledge effectively but may struggle with reasoning requiring compute intensity.

[00:46:45]  
### Fine-tuning Performance and Multilingual Training  
- FLOPs-matched sparse models (base and large) fine-tune comparably to dense models on most tasks except some ARC tasks, demonstrating robustness.  
- Multilingual training on ~100 languages shows sparsity yields strong speedups (4x) over dense mT5 models on 91 languages, suggesting experts may specialize by language.

[00:47:46]  
### Distillation of Sparse Models to Dense Models  
- Sparse models have many parameters, posing serving challenges.  
- Distillation compresses sparse models into smaller dense models by matching logits (soft labels).  
- Experiments show distilled dense models retain **~30-40% of the performance gains** of the sparse teacher models, while reducing parameter count significantly (up to 99%).  
- This offers a practical trade-off between training efficiency (sparse) and serving efficiency (dense).  
- Clarified that "percent of teacher performance" means the distilled model recovers part of the performance gap between dense and sparse models, not literal accuracy of teacher predictions.

[00:51:16]  
### Clarifications on Sparsity and Model Structure  
- Sparsity here differs from pruning or compression:  
  - Instead of zeroing weights, Switch Transformers have **many more parameters organized in blocks (experts)**.  
  - Each token activates one expert (block), enabling structured sparsity beneficial for hardware efficiency and communication.  
- Dense models with weight regularization (L1/L2) do not produce the same structured sparsity and hardware advantages.  
- The block structure is critical to efficient routing and parallelism.

[00:57:17]  
### Potential Directions and Co-design with Hardware  
- Future research includes co-designing models, parallelization strategies, and hardware.  
- Current Switch Transformer models are designed to fit well with SPMD mesh partitioning and large-scale distributed hardware.  
- Scaling hidden dimension is a key factor for balancing compute and communication costs.  
- New distributed algorithms (e.g., Microsoft ZeRO) present further opportunities for model and hardware co-design.  
- The team is optimistic about combining hardware and model design for scalable, efficient training.

[01:00:02]  
### Summary and Outlook  
- Switch Transformer is a simplified MoE variant offering:  
  - Strong speedups in pre-training (up to 7x over T5 baselines).  
  - Effective distillation to dense models preserving gains.  
  - Robust fine-tuning and multilingual capabilities.  
  - Scalability to 1.6 trillion parameters sparse models.  
- Emerging work explores sparsity and adaptive computation in vision models with promising scaling and efficiency benefits.  
- Capacity factors below one have been tested in vision, showing good performance by exploiting input redundancy and selective computation.  
- Linear attention mechanisms (e.g., Performers) have limited impact currently since attention costs are not dominant in many NLP tasks at scale.  
- The approach is scalable beyond 10 trillion parameters, limited mostly by storage and hardware availability.  
- Sparse models are expected to play a significant role in future large-scale transformer architectures, especially for pre-training and high-throughput applications.

---

### Key Terms and Definitions  

| Term                    | Definition                                                                                         |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Sparsity**            | Selective activation of parts of a model’s weights for each input, reducing computation per token.|
| **Mixture of Experts (MoE)** | Architecture with multiple expert subnetworks and a router that selects which experts to activate per input token. |
| **Switch Transformer**  | Simplified MoE variant routing each token to a single top expert to reduce communication overhead.|
| **Capacity Factor**     | Hyperparameter controlling how many tokens an expert can process relative to average load.       |
| **Selective Precision** | Training technique casting certain computations (e.g., routing softmax) in higher precision to improve stability. |
| **Load Balancing Loss** | Auxiliary loss encouraging equal token distribution across experts to improve hardware efficiency.|
| **Distillation**        | Compressing a large (teacher) model into a smaller (student) model by matching output distributions.|
| **Expert Parallelism**  | Parallelism achieved by distributing different experts (weight blocks) across devices.           |
| **Model Parallelism**   | Parallelism achieved by splitting dense model weights across devices.                             |
| **Data Parallelism**    | Parallelism achieved by replicating model weights and splitting data batches across devices.     |

---

### Summary Table: Sparse Models vs Dense Models on Tasks  

| Model Type       | Parameters       | FLOPs per Token | Fine-tuning Performance (Reasoning - SuperGLUE) | Fine-tuning Performance (Knowledge - TriviaQA) | Speedup over Dense Baseline | Notes                                      |
|------------------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Dense T5-XXL     | ~13B             | Baseline        | Better than sparse for reasoning              | Baseline                                     | 1x                         | Dense, no sparsity                         |
| Switch XL        | ~400B (64 experts)| Similar to T5-XXL| Slightly worse than dense                      | Better than dense                            | Up to 7x                   | Sparse, single-expert routing              |
| Switch C         | ~1.6T            | ~10x fewer      | Worse than Switch XL and dense                 | Best knowledge task performance              | Pareto efficient           | Extreme sparsity with reduced compute      |
| Small Expert Sparse| Few experts      | FLOPs matched   | Comparable to dense                            | Comparable                                   | Moderate                   | Efficient even at low compute regimes      |

---

### Timeline of Key Concepts and Developments  

| Timestamp  | Event / Topic                                                                                   |
|------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 00:00:05   | Introduction: Motivation for scaling transformers via sparsity                                  |
| 00:02:39   | Mixture of Experts concept and router mechanism                                                |
| 00:05:01   | Switch Transformer architecture overview and training improvements                             |
| 00:09:08   | Selective precision casting for stable training                                               |
| 00:13:01   | Load balancing loss and token routing strategies                                              |
| 00:20:36   | Research question: Why sparsity over adaptive computation?                                    |
| 00:26:02   | Performance comparison: top-1 vs top-2 routing and capacity factors                            |
| 00:29:05   | Scaling switch transformers to many experts and speedup benefits                              |
| 00:31:12   | Sparse scaling laws and comparison of parallelism types                                      |
| 00:41:46   | Large sparse model design and pre-training results                                            |
| 00:43:45   | Parameter vs compute trade-offs: reasoning vs knowledge tasks                                 |
| 00:47:46   | Distillation of sparse to dense models                                                       |
| 00:57:17   | Co-design of models and hardware for efficient parallel training                             |
| 01:00:02   | Summary, future directions, and emerging work in vision and adaptive computation             |

---

### Key Insights  
- **Sparsity introduces a new dimension of scaling that enables extremely large models with fewer FLOPs but many more parameters.**  
- **Switch Transformer’s top-1 routing balances performance and efficiency better than top-2 routing at low capacity factors.**  
- **Selective precision and initialization refinements are critical for stable training of sparse models.**  
- **Load balancing is important for hardware efficiency but must be balanced against expert specialization to maintain performance.**  
- **Sparse models excel on knowledge-heavy tasks but may lag on reasoning tasks compared to dense models with equivalent compute.**  
- **Distillation provides a practical path to deploy sparse model gains in dense architectures, helping with serving complexity.**  
- **Sparse architectures map well to current hardware and distributed frameworks, enabling scaling to trillion-parameter models.**  
- **Sparsity and adaptive computation (variable amount of compute per input) are complementary research directions.**  
- **Sparsity shows promise beyond NLP, with demonstrated benefits in computer vision through selective computation.**

---

This summary strictly reflects the video content and the research findings and insights presented therein.

name: Agentic AI
description:
### Summary  
The video presents a comprehensive overview of language models (LMs), particularly focusing on the progression from traditional language model usage to agentic language models (Agent AI). The speaker, Inso, begins by explaining the fundamental principles of language models—statistical models predicting the next word in a sequence based on large-scale pre-training on diverse corpora. Post-training methods such as instruction tuning and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) are discussed as critical enhancements that align models to human preferences and improve usability.

The talk outlines best practices for prompt engineering, emphasizing clear, descriptive instructions, few-shot examples, contextual references, reasoning chains, and task decomposition techniques to maximize LM effectiveness. It also highlights the importance of systematic logging and automated evaluation to track model performance and support iterative improvement.

Inso then explores the inherent limitations of current language models, including hallucinations (generation of incorrect information), knowledge cutoffs, lack of attribution, data privacy concerns, and limited context windows. To address these challenges, the concept of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is introduced, where external, up-to-date, and proprietary information can be indexed and selectively retrieved to improve accuracy and reduce hallucination.

The talk transitions to the concept of tool usage or function calling, where language models can generate API calls or executable code to interact with external systems (e.g., weather APIs), enabling real-time data access and complex computations beyond pure text generation.

The core of the presentation centers on agentic language models—an advanced usage paradigm where the language model acts as an “agent” interacting with its environment through iterative reasoning and action cycles. This includes breaking down complex tasks into subtasks, making API calls, retrieving data, and using memory of past interactions to produce more nuanced and context-aware outputs. Examples include customer support agents performing multi-step refund policy checks and software assistant agents iteratively debugging and testing code.

Several design patterns for agentic LMs are discussed: planning (task decomposition), reflection (self-critique and iterative improvement), tool usage (external API/function calls), and multi-agent collaboration (specialized agents handling distinct subtasks). The benefits of agentic usage include tackling more complex problems, improving output quality, and expanding the range of possible applications.

Practical advice is given on training data requirements, starting with small datasets and augmenting them with synthetic data generated by LMs. The speaker addresses evaluation strategies, recommending a layered, reflective approach to judging model outputs beyond single-pass evaluation.

Ethical considerations are also touched upon, especially the ongoing challenges of hallucination and unsafe outputs. Solutions include guardrails, output monitoring with classifiers or smaller models, and enterprise-level safety checks.

Finally, the talk recommends starting simple by experimenting with playgrounds and API calls before advancing to agentic frameworks. It encourages continuous learning from industry experts and courses to keep abreast of the rapidly evolving field.

### Highlights  
- [00:00:51] 🤖 Explanation of language models as next-word prediction systems trained on large corpora.  
- [00:07:17] ✍️ Best practices for prompt engineering: clear instructions, few-shot examples, context, reasoning chains, and task decomposition.  
- [00:21:10] ⚠️ Common limitations of language models: hallucination, knowledge cutoff, lack of attribution, privacy, and limited context windows.  
- [00:24:06] 🔍 Introduction of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to use external data and reduce hallucination.  
- [00:27:44] 🛠️ Tool usage/function calling for real-time external API interaction and code execution.  
- [00:30:01] 🤖 Agentic language models combine reasoning and action cycles, iteratively interacting with the environment and memory.  
- [00:39:40] 🔄 Design patterns for agentic LMs: planning, reflection, tool usage, and multi-agent collaboration.  

### Key Insights  
- [00:02:11] 📚 **Pre-training and Post-training Synergy:** The two-stage training process (pre-training on massive datasets followed by fine-tuning with human instructions and RLHF) is essential to build language models that are not only knowledgeable but also aligned to user intent. This layered training enhances practical usability and safety, addressing the gap between raw statistical models and useful AI assistants.  
- [00:07:59] 🧠 **Prompt Engineering as a Critical Skill:** How you frame questions or instructions (prompting) drastically impacts the quality and relevance of the model’s output. Techniques such as providing detailed instructions, few-shot examples, contextual references, and enabling chain-of-thought reasoning allow users to leverage latent model capabilities more effectively, transforming generic LMs into domain-specific tools.  
- [00:21:10] ⚠️ **Limitations Highlight Need for Hybrid Approaches:** Hallucination, outdated knowledge, and lack of source attribution pose significant risks, especially in high-stakes applications. These weaknesses emphasize that LMs alone cannot be blindly trusted and motivate augmentation with retrieval systems, external data sources, and verification mechanisms to ensure reliability.  
- [00:24:53] 🗃️ **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Enables Proprietary Data Use:** By embedding domain-specific or proprietary documents into vector databases, systems can dynamically fetch relevant information and inject it into prompts. This approach reduces hallucination, improves factuality, and bridges the gap between static model knowledge and real-time or confidential information, making LMs more practical for enterprise and specialized applications.  
- [00:30:01] 🔄 **Agentic Language Models Merge Reasoning with Action:** Unlike traditional LMs with static input-output behavior, agentic models interact in iterative loops with external tools and memory, enabling complex multi-step workflows. This paradigm shift allows AI systems to autonomously plan, execute, reflect, and adapt, thereby expanding the scope of AI applications from simple chatbots to dynamic assistants capable of task automation and decision-making.  
- [00:39:40] 🧩 **Design Patterns Foster Robust Agentic AI Development:** Patterns like planning (task breakdown), reflection (self-evaluation and improvement), tool use (external APIs or code execution), and multi-agent collaboration (specialized sub-agents) provide a framework to design scalable and maintainable AI agents. Reflection, in particular, is an effective and relatively easy-to-implement strategy that enhances output quality by iterative self-critique.  
- [00:50:44] 🛡️ **Ethical Considerations and Safety Mechanisms Are Imperative:** Hallucination and harmful content risks require layered safety measures, including model provider filtering, enterprise guardrails, and output classifiers. Continuous evaluation and ethical oversight remain critical as models grow more powerful. The probabilistic nature of language generation means that perfect safety is elusive, but systematic mitigation can reduce risks.  

This talk serves as a comprehensive guide to understanding current language model technology, its limitations, and the emerging agentic AI paradigm that integrates reasoning, action, and external tool use to unlock more sophisticated AI capabilities. It blends theoretical foundations with practical advice and real-world examples, making it an invaluable resource for developers, researchers, and AI practitioners exploring the cutting edge of language model applications.

name: Multimodal LLMs
description:
### Summary of the Talk by Ming Ding on Multimodality and Large Language Models (CS 25 Transformers)

---

#### - [00:00:05 → 00:02:21] Introduction and Speaker Background
- Ming Ding, a research scientist at Jue AI (Beijing), specializing in **multimodal generative models** and **pre-training technologies**.
- Past involvement includes projects like **Cog View**, **Cog Video**, **Cog VLM**, and **Cog Agent**.
- Talk overview: history of large language models (LLMs), practical training techniques, recent developments in vision-language (VL) models, and future research directions in multimodality.

---

#### - [00:02:21 → 00:13:11] Key Historical Milestones in Language Model Development

- **Three pivotal moments** in LLM evolution:

| Moment           | Description                                                                                                   | Impact                                                                                                  |
|------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **BERT Moment**  | Introduction of BERT and masked language modeling (MLM). Early research contrasted MLM (good for understanding) and autoregressive models (better for generation). Ming Ding’s group proposed **GLM (Generalized Language Model)** to unify MLM and autoregressive modeling in a decoder-only architecture. | Demonstrated that partial masking improves efficiency and performance, bridging BERT and GPT styles.    |
| **GPT-3 Moment** | Recognition of the **scaling law**: model performance improves predictably with increased compute, parameters, and training tokens. Architecture innovations became less crucial; engineering and scaling were dominant.                    | Shifted focus to resource allocation and compute scaling for better performance instead of new architectures. |
| **ChatGPT Moment** | Highlighted that **task adaptation is cheap**. The critical factor is the **quality of pre-training loss**, not model size. Fine-tuning (task adaptation) is relatively inexpensive compared to pre-training. | Showed fine-tuning on aligned data (e.g., InstructGPT) greatly improves human preference. Model size alone doesn’t guarantee better downstream performance if training loss is poor. |

- **Key insight:** Downstream performance correlates primarily with **pre-training loss**, not model size—meaning small models trained well can match large models trained poorly.

---

#### - [00:13:11 → 00:21:27] Transformer Architectures and Training Techniques

- Transformer adaptations used in modern LLMs:
  - Shift from **encoder-decoder** to **decoder-only** architectures to reduce redundancy.
  - Adoption of **pre-layer norm** (pre-LN) instead of post-layer norm.
  - Innovations like **rotary positional embeddings** (from Bing) and **group query attention** to improve efficiency and reduce memory.
  - Replacement of MLP with **GLU (Gated Linear Units)** and **mixture of experts** to enhance performance with fewer parameters.

- Training infrastructure:
  - Use of **DeepSpeed** (Microsoft) with **ZeRO optimizations** (ZeRO-1, ZeRO-2, ZeRO-3) to manage memory efficiently.
  - Techniques like **activation checkpointing** and **CPU offloading** enable training very large models on clusters of GPUs.
  - **Megatron** framework for tensor and pipeline parallelism to scale models beyond 100 billion parameters.

- **Long Context Modeling:**
  - Significant improvements in handling **long context windows** with **lossless full attention** beyond tens of thousands of tokens.
  - Techniques such as **context parallelism** and **ring attention** enable efficient training and inference on very long sequences.
  - This technology enables models to understand and summarize large documents or multiple documents better than traditional retrieval-based methods.

---

#### - [00:21:27 → 00:30:52] Alignment Techniques and Data Importance

- **Alignment Methods:**
  - **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):** Using high-quality, domain-specific human annotations to fine-tune models.
  - **Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF):** Rarely used outside OpenAI due to complexity; requires a reward model.
  - **Direct Preference Optimization (DPO):** A simpler alternative that uses preference pairs without a reward model, gaining popularity in open-source communities.

- **Data Quality Emphasis:**
  - Data cleaning, filtering, and synthesis are the **most critical factors** in large language model performance, often underappreciated.
  - Algorithm and architecture innovations can sometimes be replaced or mimicked by carefully prepared data.
  - Example: Complex question-answering tasks that previously required graph neural networks or MCTS can now be solved by providing **long context inputs** to large models.

---

#### - [00:30:52 → 00:43:20] Vision-Language Models and Multimodality

- Recent VL model developments:
  - **BLIP-2:** Bridges CLIP image encoders and LLMs via a learnable Query Transformer to align image and text features.
  - **LLaVA:** Uses a simple linear projection to map image encoder features into the language model input space.
  - **Cog VLM:** Enhances language ability while adding image understanding by adding new trainable vision-specific modules without altering the language model core.
  - **Cog Agent:** Designed for **high-resolution inputs** like web screenshots, uses lightweight cross-attention modules for efficiency and practical applications such as web automation.
  - Other notable models include **GM4V**, which adapts LLaVA with stride convolutions for higher resolutions and excels at Optical Character Recognition (OCR), especially in Chinese documents.

- These models demonstrate **state-of-the-art performance** in tasks like image captioning, grounding, and document OCR, and many are open sourced.

---

#### - [00:43:20 → 00:55:23] Image Generation: Autoregressive vs. Diffusion Models

- Autoregressive image generation:
  - Sequence modeling of images by tokenizing images into discrete tokens.
  - Examples: **Cog View**, **DALL·E**, **Parti**.
  - Limitations: Long sequences lead to very slow generation and information loss during image tokenization, resulting in reduced quality.

- Diffusion models:
  - Use a **noise addition and denoising process** to generate images.
  - Represent a fundamentally different self-supervised paradigm from autoregressive models.
  - Advantages:
    - Much faster image generation due to parallel processing of the entire image.
    - Better modeling of spatial relationships in images (e.g., distant pixels relate more naturally).
  - Challenges addressed in recent work (**Rel Diffusion**): noise schedules that are consistent across resolutions to improve fidelity and scaling.

- Diffusion models have become the **dominant approach for high-quality image generation**, outperforming autoregressive models in both speed and output quality.

---

#### - [00:55:23 → 01:00:01] Video Generation and Future Directions

- Video generation models:
  - Earlier work (e.g., **Sora**) used autoregressive models but had limited quality.
  - Diffusion approaches are now preferred for better quality, temporal coherence, and scalability.
  - Techniques like **3D latent encoders/decoders** help reduce flickering and improve video fidelity.

- Scaling up video generation and understanding leverages **long context modeling techniques** developed for language models.

---

#### - [01:00:01 → 01:07:23] Predictions and Research Directions for Multimodality

- **Predictions for next 1-2 years:**
  - **Grounding and scene understanding** will become cheap and accurate, enabling better AI perception of humans, objects, and environments.
  - **Video understanding** will become critical due to abundant video data but remains challenging due to hallucinations and counting errors in current models like **Germany 1.5**.
  - **Embodied AI** will grow in importance, combining multimodal understanding with physical interaction, though widespread real-world impact will require more time.
  - **Speech/audio AI** remains an underestimated yet crucial area, lagging behind language model research in terms of resources and attention.

- **Advice for researchers:**
  - Collaborate with system/engineering-focused PhD students to leverage current GPU/hardware effectively.
  - Explore new architectures and optimizers, especially as hardware evolves—there may be future competitors to Transformers.
  - Innovate in **transforming raw web data into high-quality training data**, including methods like code execution and reinforcement learning to enhance data quality.
  - Video understanding datasets and benchmarks are a high-need area for impactful research.

---

#### - [01:07:23 → 01:19:36] Q&A Highlights

- **Cost of long context windows:**  
  Increased inference time due to processing very long sequences; typically users tolerate a brief delay (~seconds to a minute) before response.

- **Data quality vs. architecture innovation:**  
  Data quality currently has a greater impact. Architecture and algorithms can sometimes be replaced by data-driven solutions injecting inductive biases.

- **Why autoregressive models lag diffusion in image generation:**  
  Diffusion models parallelize generation and better capture spatial dependencies, resulting in faster and higher-quality outputs.

- **Difference between Cog Agent and Cog VLM:**  
  Cog Agent is fine-tuned from Cog VLM with cross-attention modules to handle web screenshots at very high resolution efficiently.

- **Video understanding and physical world modeling:**  
  Current models lack supervised physical knowledge from videos; better self-supervised pretraining methods are needed for physical understanding.

- **VQA tasks with tree-structured conversation:**  
  Including all context in the input generally yields better results than procedural beam search; the model learns to reason over the entire context.

---

### Key Insights & Conclusions

- **Pre-training loss quality is the dominant factor** in downstream performance, outweighing model size or architecture complexity.
- **Scaling laws** have transformed LLM development into an engineering discipline focused on compute and data.
- **Multimodal models** are evolving rapidly, with innovations in VL alignment, high-resolution input handling, and video understanding.
- **Diffusion models dominate image and video generation** due to their speed and quality advantages over autoregressive methods.
- The future of multimodality lies in **video understanding, embodied AI, and speech**, alongside continued improvements in **data quality** and **training infrastructure**.
- Researchers should consider **data-centric approaches** and **system-level collaborations** to make impactful advances in this evolving field.

---

This summary is strictly grounded in the provided transcript, capturing the main points, technical details, and expert perspectives shared by Ming Ding during the CS 25 Transformers talk.
